Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Figure 1b explicitly labels the Lpa1 locus as OsLpa1, LOC_Os02g57400.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper names Lpa1/OsLpa1 as the low phytic acid gene affecting PA accumulation, but does not explicitly describe a protein family/domain or state 'non-enzyme/common gene'.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Results directly connect lpa1 to reduced phytic acid and altered Fe and Zn pools in grains, including lower PA-bound Fe/Zn and increased grain Fe/Zn accumulation.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Phytic acid is treated as the phosphorus storage/antinutrient compound, and Fe/Zn as mineral nutrients throughout the Results and Discussion.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Results section 3.4 shows lower PA-bound Fe/Zn and higher NA/DMA-associated Fe/Zn in lpa1 and lpa1 RcN2 grains, supporting improved mineral bioavailability rationale.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: PA, NA, and DMA were measured in mature brown grains, and mineral contents were measured in both brown and polished grains; however PA-bound Fe/Zn speciation was assessed from brown grain extracts, not polished grain specifically.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Materials and Methods states the WT cultivar Dongjin is Oryza sativa L. ssp. japonica.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Materials and Methods explicitly states the rice cultivar Dongjin was used.

Applied_Species.reason: The engineered and mutant plants were all rice.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied species in this study is Oryza sativa.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: The transformations/mutations were performed in cultivar Dongjin.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show CRISPR lpa1 lowered grain PA, lpa1/lpa1 RcN2 had lower PA-bound Fe/Zn, lpa1 grains had slightly higher NA/DMA, and mineral accumulation was highest in lpa1 RcN2.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are described and used in Results: CRISPR/Cas9 knockout, PA assay, LC-MS/MS, ICP-MS, SEC-ICP-MS/ESI-MS, field phenotyping, and SEM.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Plants were grown in growth chamber conditions and in a field paddy; field-grown plants were used for phenotypic and grain measurements.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: The paper studies the combined genotype lpa1 RcN2, but does not show a physical or molecular protein-protein interaction between OsLpa1 and OsNAS2.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each listed point matches Results: ~30–41% PA reduction, lower PA-bound Fe/Zn with slightly higher NA/DMA in lpa1, strongest mineral accumulation in lpa1 RcN2, and agronomic penalties plus chalkiness.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Discussion explicitly presents lpa1/lpa1 RcN2 as useful for improved mineral bioavailability and chalky grains favorable for flour, while suggesting combination with other low-phytate alleles to reduce yield penalties.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results section 3.6 directly reports shorter culms, fewer tillers, reduced panicle/grain traits, lower seed-setting, lower hundred-grain weight, and high chalkiness in lpa1 backgrounds.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Discussion states chalkiness may benefit flour production despite poorer conventional grain quality, and suggests combining RcN2 with alternative low-phytate alleles lacking agronomic penalties.

corrections.reason: The paper studies the combined genotype lpa1 RcN2, but does not show a physical or molecular protein-protein interaction between OsLpa1 and OsNAS2.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The paper identifies NAS2/OsNAS2 as nicotianamine synthase 2.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number is provided anywhere in the paper text.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Results state NAS2 catalyzes biosynthesis of NA, and Discussion states NA is the precursor to DMA; this supports the reaction description at a general level.

Primary_Substrate.reason: S-adenosyl-L-methionine is not mentioned in the paper text provided.

Primary_Product.reason: Results 3.1 explicitly states NAS2 catalyzes the biosynthesis of NA.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The paper centers on NA and DMA biosynthesis/metal chelator biosynthesis regulated by NAS2.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Discussion states NAS2 synthesizes NA, which is the precursor to DMA, supporting placement in the NA-to-DMA branch.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: Because NAS2 synthesizes NA and NA is precursor to DMA, NAS2 is upstream of DMA production as described in the paper.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: The paper explicitly links NAS2-derived NA to downstream DMA production and to increased grain mineral accumulation.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: NAS2 is clearly important in this study, but the paper does not explicitly call it rate-limiting or a key control step.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Results directly connect OsNAS2 overexpression to increased NA and DMA and to increased Fe and Zn in grains.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: NA/DMA are treated as metal chelators and Fe/Zn as mineral nutrients throughout the paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Results and Discussion state NA and DMA chelate metals, promote transport to grains, and are associated with more bioavailable Fe/Zn forms.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: NA/DMA were measured in mature brown grains, minerals in brown and polished grains, and Discussion/Results infer enhanced endosperm enrichment after polishing.

Source_Species.reason: OsNAS2 is a rice gene and the study is in rice.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The study species is Oryza sativa L. ssp. japonica.

Applied_Species.reason: The transgenic lines were generated in rice.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied species is Oryza sativa.

Applied_Promoters.reason: RcN2 is explicitly described as OsRCc3pro:NAS2, with OsRCc3 as a tissue-specific/root-predominant promoter.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Results show higher NAS2 transcript levels in roots of RcN2 and lpa1 RcN2, and figure legend/discussion describe OsRCc3 as predominantly active in roots and also in node phloem companion cells.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: No subcellular localization for OsNAS2 is stated in the paper text.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show root-directed NAS2 overexpression increased grain NA/DMA, raised Fe/Zn/Mn/Cu and also Mg/K, increased NA/DMA-bound Fe/Zn, and RcN2 showed no major agronomic penalty.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: The listed methods are all explicitly used: transgenic overexpression under OsRCc3, RT-qPCR, LC-MS/MS, ICP-MS, SEC-ICP-MS/ESI-MS, and field phenotyping.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Growth chamber and field-grown paddy conditions are explicitly described.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: OsNAAT1, OsDMAS1, OsTOM1, OsIRT1, OsYSL2, OsCOPT1, OsZIP4, and OsZIP5 were measured as co-regulated genes, not demonstrated as interacting proteins with OsNAS2.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All four points are directly supported by the Results: strong NA/DMA increase, higher minerals in brown/polished grains, stronger effect when combined with lpa1 including speciation shift, and no significant agronomic penalty in RcN2.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Discussion explicitly frames RcN2/OsNAS2 as a promising biofortification resource, especially with tissue-specific expression and future introgression into elite cultivars.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results show no significant penalty in RcN2 alone, while lpa1 RcN2 has penalties; Discussion attributes these mainly to lpa1-related tradeoffs.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Discussion explicitly states NA/DMA were quantified only in brown grains and that contributions of root- versus phloem/node-derived chelators to grain accumulation remain speculative.

corrections.reason: No EC number is provided anywhere in the paper text.
S-adenosyl-L-methionine is not mentioned in the paper text provided.
No subcellular localization for OsNAS2 is stated in the paper text.

corrections.old_value: OsNAAT1; OsDMAS1; OsTOM1; OsIRT1; OsYSL2; OsCOPT1; OsZIP4; OsZIP5
corrections.reason: OsNAAT1, OsDMAS1, OsTOM1, OsIRT1, OsYSL2, OsCOPT1, OsZIP4, and OsZIP5 were measured as co-regulated genes, not demonstrated as interacting proteins with OsNAS2.
